sentences.text: Julie, you wouldn't bother me.
I was telling you about the Lodz Ghetto.
And the food.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I used to walk the other way to cross to get past the thoroughfares.
I don't know whether you have seen at the Holocaust Museum the big bridge in the Lodz Ghetto.
I used to walk along the barbed wire fence for about 10 blocks.
It was very dangerous on the street because you never knew who might want to just take a potshot shot at you and kill you.

Up those stairs in 20, 30 degrees below, slip and slide, cross over to the other side, walk along the fence again till I got to a distribution center, pick up a sack of, I don't know, probably 30, 40 pounds of potatoes, sling it on my back, take it home.
Now, this might have to last us three to six months.
The thing that I remember the most about the large ghetto are death wagons.
They were a daily routine.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And now you get used to anything.
Usually pulled by human beings, men, two.
How they knew where the bodies were, I don't know.
I had a girlfriend who died of tuberculosis.
She looked so healthy when I met her.
I couldn't believe we-- we weren't used to pink cheeks in the ghetto.
We all looked like skeletons.
And she had the brightest red cheeks.
And one day she started coughing.
And I took a look at her, and it was pink foam, and then it turned to blood just pouring, trickling down.
Within about three, four months she died.
The TB had spread to her bones, to every organ.
See, we had doctors, but we had no medication.
So we knew what the disease was, but there was no cure, no treatment.
And when I went to see her after work her mother opened the door-- her name was Esther-- and I said, how is she today?
The mother said, oh, she died.
I said, could I see her?
She says, no, the death wagon came and took her away already.
It was like a cart, a large cart pulled by two human beings.
And they would go into an apartment, a room, I mean-- we all lived in single rooms-- carry out the body, throw it on a wagon.
No shroud, no coffin.
Nothing.
And when the cart was so full that the bodies were falling off it, then they would take it to the cemetery and come back.
So what I remember are arms hanging out of the cart, heads rolled back, stiff legs sticking out.
We become just immune to everything, you know?
I saw this every day of my life in the ghetto, towards the end three, four, five times a day.
As the war progressed and the Russian front was drawing nearer, they started to liquidate the ghetto in all seriousness.
Up until then we were valuable to them, the workers.
They were just eliminating the sick, the children, the elderly.
Some people did.
It was word of mouth.
I'm sure some had radios hidden or managed to get in and out of the ghetto.
There was communication.
I did not.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm sure my mother did.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was busy working, still being a kid.
We had a library and I used to go to the library, check out books and read.
My big pastime was knitting a dress for myself or a skirt or a sweater-- we were very limited on clothing-- and then taking it apart, knitting something else.
You were basically unraveling other people's clothing, whatever we could lay our hands on. 1944-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the round ups were occurring daily now.
Factories were being closed down, from spring on especially.
Ours was still going.
As long as we had a job we were safe.

And then we got word again through my mother's friend that we were not to come to work because the factory would not reopen and that they were going to ship everybody out as they showed up for work for transport.
They would just round them up.
And there was a hiding place a few houses over, buildings over from ours.
And there was room for myself and my mother if we wanted to go.
So we did.
We took very little.
I wore three of those knit dresses.
I think we each took a pillow and, I don't know, my blanket or something, went to the house.
There was a trap door to a cellar that had been dug underneath the living room.
Someone opened the trap door.
They had arranged for one person to come once a day during the middle of the night and open it for us from the outside.
For safety's sake, they had put a little area rug and a rocking chair above this opening so we wouldn't be discovered.
I can't tell you how many people.
There were no lights, dark, moldy earth.
I slept most of the time.
I didn't know if it was night or day.
Someone would come let us out to use the outhouse.
I think maybe there was a chamber pot in there in a corner.
Get some fresh air-- one at a time we'd go back in.
After more than a week-- I know that-- a young man came and it was at a different time, and we thought we were found, that our hiding place had been discovered.
He was a messenger.
Our friend had sent him.
The Germans had promised they were now totally liquidating the ghetto.

The name Rumkowski, who was the president of the ghetto and the government, had agreed to leave on a passenger train that was being provided for them, the factory heads, the police, and their families, whoever was left in the ghetto-- safe transport to Germany to work in a factory.
The message was they had accepted.
If we wanted to continue hiding there would be no one to help us.
So we all decided to go.
As soon as we reached the train station [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we knew it was wrong.
SS officers, police dogs, billy clubs, sub machine guns, cattle train.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They forced aboard this train, probably 100 people in each car.
There was no room to stretch your legs out.
You could sit down almost in a fetal position, like this, curled up, your feet under you, then the next person, the next.
Well, by then I was 14 and I had a very good, straight upbringing.
I had become the provider and the procurer.
So I managed to quickly get a little area against one of the walls of the car so at least we had something to lean against.
And my mother and I sat down there.
One more time, the same scenario-- slam the doors, lock 'em, off we went.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Early, early one morning, the train stopped.
[GERMAN] Schnell!
Schnell!
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were cramped from sitting like that.
There wasn't even a chamber pot by then.
We were sitting in our own feces and urine.
People had diarrhea, dysentery, vomiting.
I remember at least three bodies dead.
How many more in the other part of the car, I don't know.
We were forced to jump down and immediately they separated the men from the women, the women and children walking one direction, men in the opposite direction.
So it was like this way, split.
Stood single file, and I saw a sign, and it was such a pretty, black rod iron gate, very ornate-- 'Arbeit macht frei.' Work makes one free.
And underneath it someplace I saw this sign, a little, like, a train city sign-- [NON-ENGLISH].
I had no idea.
I just thought we had arrived in a town named Auschwietchem to go to work.
And then I heard whispers down the line, Auschwitz, Auschwitz.
Auschwietchem, of course, as we all now know was Auschwitz.
That was the town.
I realized right away that they were sorting, separating us.
You had to go past their review committee.
There were three SS uniformed men.
I've heard since that Dr.
Mengele was always one of them.
I had no idea who they were.
And I noticed that the elderly, young children, and women with infants were all being sent to the left.
All the younger, stronger, able-bodied women were going to the right.
Well, I was in front of my mother.
And I was still wearing the three dresses.
I still had the long hair.
Here's the ironic part of it.
I was too young for the labor side of Auschwitz.
I knew I had to try and pass for 16.
I don't know if that was the age, but that was the magical number.
I probably weighed 70 pounds by then from malnutrition.
I was not developed, maybe four feet seven or eight.
But I had a piece of string with me.
And I tied my hair on the very end, in the back, then pulled it under and tied it around here into a pageboy that filled my face in.
Don't ask me what I looked like, filthy, bedraggled, right-- three dresses which filled me out.
And when I got to the review committee I was so bound and determined, stood as straight as I could and I just smiled at them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know what made me do it.
With the flick of one finger [POINTS] my fate was decided.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He went to the right.
I passed.
My mother passed.
We were in Auschwitz.
They marched us in, told us we were filthy, which of course we were.
And we were going to be taken to the showers to get cleaned.
By then, everybody knew what the showers meant.
There were stories circling.
Nobody could believe it, but we knew, even though you know the mind could not comprehend it.
So they marched us to the showers, told us to take all of our clothing off, that we would pick it up on the other side.
They'd fumigate it.
We stood naked for a long time in a snake-like line.
And I remember when I reached the end of that line and could see, there was a long, narrow room with mirrors on both sides, and chairs standing in front of these mirrors.
Behind each mirror was an inmate, a Jewish female in a uniform.
She had-- They had hair.
They were pretty.

You sat down in the chair, within 30 seconds they shaved all of my hair-- and in my case it wasn't necessary but they did it anyhow-- under the armpits, all your pubic hair, and they had a bucket of green slime next to them with a little short-handled mop.
Strange how some things you remember-- I mean, it took maybe one minute.

After she shaved me, she pulled out this mop and poured this green slime on my head, under my armpits, in the pubic area. 'Raus.' You got out of the chair and you went forward to the other end of the room.
Somehow or other my mother and I got separated.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, I was short, so I even had a more difficult time.
When they thought they had enough of us, I can't tell you how many, they proceeded to beat the people at the very back.
They opened this humongous door.
I think it was an overhead door.
Again, these are just impressions.
And by beating the people in a back, because I remember the screaming and the crying, those people tried to get away from it, and they pushed us in forward.
So there I went with a mob.
There wasn't even a place to truly fall down.
We were body to body.
Lock the doors behind us, and people were crying, screaming, praying.
I didn't know what it was then but I remember them saying, [NON-ENGLISH].
I did not understand the Jewish prayers, but I do remember those words.
And then I heard a tremendous hiss.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And, of course, bedlam.
Everybody started screaming, it's the gas.
And the one thought I had, I'm going to die without saying goodbye to my mother.
Where is she?
And then I felt a drop of water on me, and another, and they were almost like the sprinkling systems, you know with, for fire.
They opened up and the water poured down on us.
It poured for a minute, two, I don't know.
Jubilation.
Another set of doors opened on the opposite side.
Out we went into a very large enclosure, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 30 feet around, maybe three to four feet high, maybe five, with women standing on it.
It was full of clothing and shoes.
And there was like a chicken wire or a little cyclone fence around it.
And we were marching past.
We had to go past there.
As we walked past a woman threw each one of us an item of clothing and a pair of shoes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They threw me a little girl's dress.
Fortunately, I was tiny.
It hit me here, [POINTS] like a micro-mini, navy blue with a very fine pinstripe, a white pinstripe just running through it, and a white round collar on it.
Sleeves hit me here.
They were supposed to be long.
But it fit my body.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then another woman threw me a pair of shoes.
They were this big.
High heels, the clunker heels.
About an inch and a half heel.
That was my uniform till the day I was liberated.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No underwear.
Nothing else.
I started looking for my mother and I saw this slight little figure in a pair of men's long johns.
She turned around.
She looked at me, I looked at her.
You know, without our hair and clothing we looked so different, yet we did recognize each other.
So I took off.
I just ran straight to her.
We grabbed each other.
Whether it was a relief at being alive, sense of humor that neither one of us lost throughout the whole thing, or hysteria, we started laughing.
We just hold each other back, look at each other and peal with laughter.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
From there we were lined up, counted, stood at attention for hours, marched to our new home, the barracks, walked in.
There were the three-tier bunkers.
You've seen many of them.
I'm sure everybody has.
What, two feet of space in between each tier, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe the size of a card chair seat per person, maybe four feet deep.
I remember my mother and I were in a middle tier.
I remember having to sit down and scoot back on my behind to get in there.
We didn't go out much.
It was dangerous.
Only went if you had to use the latrines.
Now, I've heard that there were women's bathrooms and men's restrooms.
I was in lager A.
We didn't have any.
I remember a huge hole with a wooden plank with holes cut out in it.
That was the latrine out in the open.
We stood at attention twice a day, sometimes as long as three or four hours at a time without moving while they counted us.
In the morning, inmates would bring in a garbage can full of lukewarm, brackish brown water.
It was called coffee.
And each one of us received a very small chunk, slice, of black bread.
The rest of the day was nothing.
We were never in a labor camp.
I guess we were in a holding pen.
Again, about four PM-- this was about 4:30 AM when the coffee would come, maybe four.
Then we would go out, stand at attention, be counted.
In between, they would do lineups and segregation, perpetually selecting people for gas chambers, every single day.
We'd have to stand naked.
It was cold by then, raining, mud, barefoot.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Around four in the afternoon they'd bring in another garbage can full of what was called soup.
This time gray, lukewarm water with a few pieces, little, maybe one inch cube chunk of potato, rotten piece of cabbage, rotten piece of parsnip or kohlrabi.
That was it.
That was our daily rations.
I got dysentery.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother wasn't doing well.
She'd had a heart condition.
But we were still together and still alive.
And then one day we went through another line up segregation.
And I was spattered with mud all the way up the back of my legs.
They took one look at me-- I didn't think my mother would make it.
She was weak.
So this time we reversed.
Mother stood in front of me.
It seems that it was always possible to follow a person to what we guessed was the extermination side.
But you could never come from that side to the still alive side.
I was positive that if one of us was chosen for the gas chamber, it would be my mother.
So she went first so I could go after her.
I didn't want to live without her.
She passed.
I didn't.
However, it turned out I wasn't going to the gas chamber.
We were marched to another lager, lager C.
It was an all Hungarian.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was told that if I cried and begged the head couple might take pity on me, since neither one of us had been selected for the gas chamber, and maybe bring my mother to me or take me back to lager A.
I threw myself in the mud.
It was pouring.
I cried, and I cried, and I screamed.
Nothing happened.
Pretty soon I couldn't stop it.
I lost all self-control.
Evidently, I must have passed out at a certain point because when I came to I was laying on a dirt ground covered in a tent type of an area.
And there were Polish voices around me.
Six Polish Jewesses had heard my screams and they waited till the cover of darkness, went and dragged me inside and proceeded to protect me.
They bathed me.
I had dysentery and I was so debilitated I couldn't stand up.
I'd just keel over.
Would you believe in that hell hole, six young women, each one gave up one piece of her potato for me?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We formed a group.
Two of them had belonged to the Communist party, three of them, actually, in the Lodz Ghetto.
I really didn't know what communism was.
But to me it was beautiful.
It was true form of communism.
One for all, all for one.
Share, share alike.
And they did.
And with that one extra bite of their potato, which multiplied, was six bites of either potato or cabbage or whatever, I regained sufficient strength.
They held me up during the countdowns and all, that they support me.
They surrounded me.
As I started falling they'd push me, every which way.
I got strong enough and there was a transport.
We didn't know where to.
We volunteered and we were all selected.
